text_a: Malaquez's spot opened up an' showed a greenmunk trapped in like, solid air. Bits of leaf mold was flyin' from his feet as he ran up to meet a homie or someone bringin' food. Tasha was all amazed, like "ooh." I was like, "Frodo been comin' 'round here, huh?" Malaquez was like, "Yo pet?" I'm like, "Nah, he just be around. Prob'ly came 'cause of all that noise up the hill." Malaquez was like, "Ah." Then he's like, "Why 'Frodo'?" Tasha said, "Little dude with big, furry feet. What else you gon' call him?" She passed that sculpture to me. I almost dropped that thing; thought it'd be light as a holo. "Heavy," I said, like he ain't know. He cracked up. "My last piece was four old heads by a trash fire. Be glad ain't nobody thrown that at you." He was all excited 'bout his art like a lil' kid. "Um, I should wait to holla at you, but..." Dat sculpture heavy?

"The side of Malaquez's parcel gave way to reveal a greenmunk caught in a sheen of solid air. Bits of leaf mold flew from under his feet as he ran to greet a friend or a bringer of food. Tasha oohed in awe. I said, "Frodo's been visiting you, huh?" Malaquez said, "Your pet?" "Hardly. He lives around here somewhere. I suppose he was attracted to the commotion up the hill." "Ah," Malaquez said. "Why 'Frodo'?" Tasha said, "A little fellow with big, furry feet. What else could he be called?" She handed the sculpture to me. I almost dropped it; I expected it to weigh no more than a holo. "Heavy," I said, as if he might not have known. He laughed. "My last piece was of four old Undersiders crouched around a trash fire. Be glad someone didn't toss that to you." He spoke of his art with the enthusiasm of a seven-year-old. "Um, I should wait to importune you, but..." Dat sculpture heavy?

Tasha was all amazed, like "ooh." I was like, "Frodo been coming by your place, huh?" Malaquez was like, "Your pet?" "Nah, he just be around here. Guess he came by 'cause of all the noise up the hill." "Ah," Malaquez said. "Why 'Frodo'?" Tasha was like, "A little dude with big, furry feet. What else you gonna call him?" She passed that sculpture to me. I nearly let it drop; thought it'd be light as a holo. "Heavy," I mentioned, like he might not have peeped. He cracked up. "My last work was four old heads from down below huddled 'round a trash fire. Be thankful nobody threw that your way." He talked 'bout his art like he was still seven. "Um, I should wait to bother you, but..." It heavy?

"The Finnish government and the big money folks who loaned to the broke Waertsilae Marine Industries Oy done agreed in principle to start a new company to finish most of the struggling shipyard's 15 ships backlog. This new company gonna try to cut down on the shipyard's losses, folks involved said. "The deal is, the bankruptcy court gonna step out the shipbuilding game. Everything gonna be handled by the new company," said Christian Andersson, executive vice president of Oy Waertsilae, the old head of Waertsilae Marine. Once they lock down who owns it, the new company gonna start talks with state-appointed receivers to buy or rent Waertsilae Marine's shipyard spots. Subcontractors gonna get a settlement offer and they expect a quick switch to new bosses to keep skilled workers from dipping out of Waertsilae Marine's two big shipyards, government folks said. Under a deal signed yesterday, the government and Union Bank of Finland gonna be the big bosses in the new company, each putting in 100 million Finnish markkaa ($23.5 million). Oy Waertsilae gonna throw in 200 million markkaa, most of it as subordinated debt, and take a smaller piece of The Finnish government gon' own the new company?

"The Finnish government and big lenders of bankrupt shipyard Waertsilae Marine Industries Oy done agreed in principle to start up a new company to finish most of the troubled shipyard's backlog of 15 ships. The new company gonna try to limit the shipyard's losses, participants said. "The situation is the bankruptcy court gonna step out the shipbuilding business. Everything gonna be taken over by the new company," said Christian Andersson, executive vice president of Oy Waertsilae, former parent of Waertsilae Marine. Once its ownership is locked down, the new company gonna start talks with state-appointed receivers to buy or lease Waertsilae Marine's shipyard facilities. Subcontractors gonna be offered a settlement and a quick switch to new management is expected to stop a mass departure of skilled workers from Waertsilae Marine's two big shipyards, government officials said. Under an accord signed yesterday, the government and Union Bank of Finland gonna become major shareholders in the new company, each putting in 100 million Finnish markkaa ($23.5 million). Oy Waertsilae gonna throw in 200 million markkaa, most of it as subordinated debt, and take a minority stake in the new company.
